query: How many traffic accidents occurred these past years?

response.headline: Serious Traffic Accidents in Reykjavík Have Plummeted
response.lede: Everyone wants to feel safer on the road, whether driving or walking. The numbers from the past twenty years tell a surprisingly hopeful story: traffic accidents with injuries or deaths have dropped by almost half since the early 2000s, and serious injuries are much rarer than before.

story_blocks.body: People are much less likely to be hurt in a car crash than they were twenty years ago.

story_blocks.heading: Fewer Accidents Per Car Than Ever Before
story_blocks.body: In the chart below, you can follow how accidents per thousand vehicles have changed since 2000. In the early 2000s, the rate was almost 50 accidents per 1,000 cars, but by 2019 it had dropped to around 26. That’s about half as many accidents per car as there used to be.

story_blocks.heading: Number of Cars Rose, Then Fell—But Accidents Still Dropped
story_blocks.body: Take a look at how the total number of vehicles on the road changed over time. The number climbed quickly at first, reaching a peak in 2007, before dropping by about 15% and then leveling off. Even though there have been ups and downs in how many cars we have, the steady fall in accident rates suggests drivers are sharing the road more safely.

story_blocks.heading: Sharp Fall in Injuries and Deadly Crashes
story_blocks.body: The next chart shows how injuries in traffic accidents have dropped over time. In 2000, there were nearly 8 injuries per 1,000 vehicles. By 2019, it was down to less than 3. Deaths from traffic accidents also went from several each year at the start of the period to none in the most recent years. For local families, this means far fewer lives interrupted by tragedy.

story_blocks.heading: Life on the Streets Feels Safer
story_blocks.body: This steady drop in accidents and injuries means roads in Reykjavík are safer for all of us. Fewer serious crashes can give peace of mind to parents, cyclists, and drivers alike. While there will always be risks, the numbers show that city life is becoming a little less dangerous every year.

response.data_note: This story is based on numbers reported by Reykjavík police from 2000 to 2019. The data does not include minor property-only accidents that drivers reported directly to their insurance, so the true number of all fender-benders is higher. Also, licence information for this data could not be fully confirmed. These numbers mostly reflect the most serious collisions—the ones that matter most for public safety.
